symptoms.symptom_name: Witnessed boiling-water scald to the anterior thigh about one hour ago

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A witnessed thermal mechanism with immediate skin injury establishes a thermal burn.
Clean boiling water excludes a caustic/acidic mechanism.

symptoms.symptom_name: Large intact thin-walled fluid-filled blisters over erythema

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Blistering over erythema is characteristic of a partial-thickness (second-degree) depth.
Full-thickness burns are typically dry, non-blistering and leathery rather than blistered.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe pain with hypersensitivity to touch and to air currents over the burn
symptoms.character: continuous burning, air-current and touch hypersensitive

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exposed, irritated superficial nerve endings make partial-thickness burns exquisitely painful.
Preserved severe pain argues against full-thickness injury, where the burn is characteristically painless.

symptoms.symptom_name: No numb, white, waxy or leathery area within the burn (uniformly red and painful)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of any painless white/leathery eschar makes a full-thickness component unlikely — but in-person confirmation is still required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Brisk blanching of the erythema on pressure (intact dermal capillary refill)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Blanching with brisk refill confirms a viable dermal capillary bed = partial thickness; a clinician-performed test, not obtainable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Total body surface area involvement under 10% (measured ~1.5%)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Small TBSA keeps this within outpatient minor-burn management and below fluid-resuscitation/transfer thresholds; requires in-person mapping.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia and mildly elevated blood pressure (HR 110, BP 145/90) from pain and distress

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pain/distress sinus tachycardia and reactive hypertension — supportive of severe pain but not diagnostic; clinician-measured, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Correct early first aid — 20 minutes of cool running water applied

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Optimal first aid limits burn-depth progression and improves outcome — a management-relevant positive, not a diagnostic discriminator.
Does not change the burn diagnosis but drives an in-person tetanus-prophylaxis decision for a barrier-breaching wound.

symptoms.symptom_name: No spreading erythema, fever or purulent discharge at presentation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of advancing erythema, systemic features or discharge at one hour makes established infection unlikely — but it is the key delayed complication to safety-net.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Blistering + severe pain + a red, blanching, non-leathery bed together place the depth at superficial partial-thickness and argue against a full-thickness injury.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Direct in-person clinical visualisation and depth/TBSA assessment
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The definitive 'investigation' for a burn is a hands-on clinical examination establishing depth (blanching/pinprick) and TBSA to confirm outpatient suitability — must be same-day.
Not indicated for a fresh, uninfected burn; reserve for later signs of infection. Ordering it now is over-investigation.
A small superficial partial-thickness burn needs no blood tests or imaging; there is no systemic involvement to work up.
